title: DeepCormack: Fermi surface tomography using model-based data-driven algorithms
summary: The experimental reconstruction of the 3D two-photon momentum density (TPMD) via angular correlation of electron-positron annihilation radiation (ACAR) is a particularly useful method for studying material Fermi surfaces. It does not rely on low temperatures, UHV conditions, or strong magnetic fields, and enables the study of the spin-resolved electronic structure of materials. Yet, it remains a challenging inverse problem. Typically, 10^8 positron annihilation events are measured for 3--6 projections of the TPMD at different angles. The standard reconstruction approach is an ACAR adaptation of Cormack's method (the MCM) that leverages the inherent symmetry in the crystal's structure. However, the poor signal-to-noise ratio means collecting data of sufficient quality for Fermi surface studies can take months per sample. We present DeepCormack, a family of data-driven model-based reconstruction algorithms that augments the MCM by integrating supervised deep-learning models (CNN, MLP, and UNet) at various stages. To overcome the lack of large experimental training sets, we propose a method which leverages singular value decomposition with dynamic mode decomposition to generate realistic synthetic TPMD volumes, requiring only a single reference momentum density computed via density functional theory. On test data, DeepCormack improves reconstruction quality over MCM by about 8.5 dB PSNR at 200M counts and remains stable at reduced counts, enabling significantly faster acquisition times. Generalisation to experimental data depends strongly on how well the training distribution from the reference momentum density matches the sample. We therefore recommend pairing DeepCormack with a DFT calculation of the target material to create sample-specific training data. Our proposed method offers either much higher quality reconstructions, or enables significantly faster ones, on the order of weeks.

AI.tldr: DeepCormack uses deep learning (CNN, MLP, UNet) to augment traditional Cormack method for reconstructing 3D two-photon momentum density from ACAR experiments, enabling faster Fermi surface studies.
AI.motivation: ACAR experiments for studying Fermi surfaces require months of data collection due to poor signal-to-noise ratio, limiting practical applications. Current reconstruction methods (MCM) are slow but rely on crystal symmetry.
AI.method: Proposed DeepCormack framework integrates supervised deep learning models at various stages of the MCM reconstruction. Synthetic training data generated via singular value decomposition with dynamic mode decomposition from single DFT reference, overcoming lack of experimental datasets.
AI.result: DeepCormack improves reconstruction quality by ~8.5 dB PSNR over MCM at 200M counts and remains stable at reduced counts. Enables acquisition times of weeks instead of months while maintaining quality.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning augmentation significantly improves TPMD reconstruction. Best performance requires sample-specific training using DFT of target material. Trade-off between higher quality or faster acquisition depending on application needs.

title: Synthesis of the Elusive Bulk Iodide Double-Perovskite Semiconductor, Cs2AgBiI6: Microcrystals and Photoconductive Films
comment: 25 pages, 7 figures + TOC graphic, additional supporting information of 25 pages, 28 figures, 2 tables. Submitted to the Journal of the American Chemical Society (JACS)
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) iodide double-perovskite (elpasolite) semiconductors have attracted interest as potential lead-free metal-halide absorber layers for solar applications. Although studied extensively by computational methods, they have remained largely inaccessible synthetically, consistent with their predicted thermodynamic instability. Here, we report the first synthesis of bulk Cs2AgBiI6, demonstrating both microcrystalline and thin-film forms. Microcrystalline powders of Cs2AgBiI6 were prepared via anion exchange from phase-pure Cs2AgBiBr6 microcrystals. The resulting iodide elpasolite shows broad absorption throughout the visible with a 1.70 +/- 0.05 eV optical bandgap and near-infrared photoluminescence centered at 1.03 eV. We identify the elimination of trace moisture in bulk Cs2AgBiBr6 as the critical factor enabling complete halide exchange and isolation of bulk Cs2AgBiI6 with phase purity. In inert atmosphere, microcrystalline Cs2AgBiI6 shows no decomposition when stored for months at room temperature or heated to ~70 °C, and it appears equally stable in dry air. Building upon these insights, we then demonstrate the preparation of phase-pure Cs2AgBiI6 films by flash thermal evaporation of Cs2AgBiBr6 followed by anion exchange. Photoconductivity measurements on such Cs2AgBiI6 films demonstrate photocarrier generation and transport, marking the first optoelectronic measurement on this elusive 3D iodide double perovskite.

AI.tldr: First synthesis and characterization of bulk Cs2AgBiI6, a lead-free 3D iodide double-perovskite semiconductor, demonstrating both microcrystalline and thin-film forms with potential for solar applications.
AI.motivation: Three-dimensional iodide double-perovskite semiconductors have been extensively studied computationally but remained inaccessible synthetically due to predicted thermodynamic instability. This research aimed to develop a viable synthesis route for lead-free metal-halide absorber layers for solar applications.
AI.method: Microcrystalline powders of Cs2AgBiI6 were prepared via anion exchange from phase-pure Cs2AgBiBr6 microcrystals, with critical attention to eliminating trace moisture. Phase-pure Cs2AgBiI6 films were prepared by flash thermal evaporation of Cs2AgBiBr6 followed by anion exchange.
AI.result: Cs2AgBiI6 shows broad absorption throughout the visible spectrum with a 1.70 ± 0.05 eV optical bandgap and near-infrared photoluminescence centered at 1.03 eV. The material demonstrates excellent stability in inert atmosphere (no decomposition when stored for months at room temperature or heated to ~70 °C) and in dry air. Photoconductivity measurements confirmed photocarrier generation and transport.
AI.conclusion: The elimination of trace moisture in bulk Cs2AgBiBr6 is critical for complete halide exchange and isolation of phase-pure Cs2AgBiI6. This work represents the first successful synthesis and optoelectronic characterization of the elusive 3D iodide double perovskite, opening pathways for its application in lead-free perovskite solar cells.

title: Multiwavelength Raman investigation of mono- and few-layer MoS2 grown by Pulsed Laser Deposition on SiO2
comment: 30 pages, 7 figures, and 1 table, including 4 pages of Supporting Information. Corresponding authors: Alice Cartoceti, Andrea Li Bassi
summary: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS$_2$) is a semiconductor whose vibrational and excitonic properties are highly sensitive to layer number and structural disorder. We demonstrate the growth of MoS$_2$ monolayers on inert, electronics-compatible SiO$_2$ substrates using room-temperature pulsed laser deposition (PLD). Control of the process parameters enables tuning from monolayer to multilayer films, which we investigate by multiwavelength Raman spectroscopy. The evolution of the Raman-shift difference between the $E_{2g}^{1}$ and $A_{1g}$ modes, combined with an assessment of defect density, tracks film growth as a function of the number of deposition laser pulses. Although excitonic effects strongly influence the optical response of two-dimensional transition-metal dichalcogenides, experimental reports of symmetry-selective exciton-phonon coupling remain limited. We provide experimental evidence of symmetry-dependent exciton-phonon coupling in PLD-grown monolayer MoS$_2$. Specifically, we observe modulation of the resonant behaviour of the out-of-plane $A_{1g}$ and in-plane $E_{2g}^{1}$ modes, related to their different coupling to A excitons, predominantly derived from Mo $d_{z^2}$ orbitals, and C excitons, characterized by mixed orbital contributions from Mo $d_{z^2}$ and S $p_x$ and $p_y$ states. Comparison with mechanically exfoliated monolayers reveals the role of growth-induced defects in modulating these interactions. These findings establish room-temperature PLD as a viable approach for growing two-dimensional MoS$_2$ on inert, electronics-compatible substrates and provide insight into the interplay between excitonic resonances and growth-induced disorder in two-dimensional MoS$_2$.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates room-temperature pulsed laser deposition (PLD) for growing MoS₂ monolayers on SiO₂ substrates, showing symmetry-dependent exciton-phonon coupling and the role of growth-induced defects in modulating excitonic properties.
AI.motivation: MoS₂ is a semiconductor with vibrational and excitonic properties highly sensitive to layer number and structural disorder. Previous methods for growing 2D MoS₂ often required high temperatures or specific substrates, limiting electronics compatibility. Additionally, experimental evidence of symmetry-selective exciton-phonon coupling remained limited despite strong excitonic effects in 2D transition-metal dichalcogenides.
AI.method: Growth of MoS₂ films on SiO₂ substrates using room-temperature pulsed laser deposition (PLD), with control of layer number via deposition laser pulses. Characterization through multiwavelength Raman spectroscopy, tracking the Raman-shift difference between E₂g¹ and A₁g modes and assessing defect density.
AI.result: Successfully grew tunable monolayer to multilayer MoS₂ films on inert SiO₂ substrates. Observed symmetry-dependent exciton-phonon coupling: out-of-plane A₁g and in-plane E₂g¹ modes show different coupling to A excitons (Mo dz² orbitals) and C excitons (mixed Mo dz² and S px, py states). Growth-induced defects were found to modulate these interactions, as revealed by comparison with mechanically exfoliated monolayers.
AI.conclusion: Room-temperature PLD is a viable approach for growing 2D MoS₂ on inert, electronics-compatible substrates. This work provides insight into the interplay between excitonic resonances and growth-induced disorder in 2D MoS₂, establishing a foundation for further development of 2D material-based electronics.

title: Cooper pairing with the onsite exchange interaction: A possible mechanism of high-temperature superconductivity
summary: Among the various mechanisms proposed for unconventional superconductivity, this paper focuses on the Coulomb interaction responsible for $d$-wave and $s\pm$-wave pairing symmetries in cuprates and iron pnictides. Although the effective interaction $U_\text{eff}=U-J$ is predominantly repulsive, an attractive component arising from the Hund's coupling parameter $J$ is sufficient to bind fractional charges. Evaluating this binding energy within a single-band Hubbard model yields a superconducting pairing gap $Δ_0$ and estimates the transition temperature $T_c$. Given the complex electronic structure and vast compositional space of these materials, the model focuses exclusively on the doped superconducting plane hosting these fractional charges. Through this approach, an analytical expression dependent on the Hubbard $U$ and Hund $J$ parameters that accurately reproduces the superconducting dome is derived. Furthermore, the model successfully addresses the characteristic electron and hole doping asymmetry observed in cuprates by accounting for Hund's coupling parameters. Finally, while the theory accurately describes strange metal behavior, it currently provides only a qualitative explanation for the pseudogap phase and the underdoped isotope effect.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for unconventional superconductivity in cuprates and iron pnictides, where the effective interaction U_eff = U - J (with J being Hund's coupling) creates an attractive component sufficient to bind fractional charges. The model derives an analytical expression for the superconducting gap Δ₀ and T_c that reproduces the superconducting dome and addresses electron/hole doping asymmetry.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanism of unconventional superconductivity in cuprates and iron pnictides, specifically how Coulomb repulsion can still lead to pairing through Hund's coupling, and to explain key experimental features like the superconducting dome and doping asymmetry.
AI.method: Single-band Hubbard model focusing on the doped superconducting plane, evaluating binding energy from Hund's coupling parameter J, with analytical derivation dependent on Hubbard U and Hund J parameters.
AI.result: Analytical expression for superconducting pairing gap Δ₀ and T_c that accurately reproduces the superconducting dome; successfully explains electron/hole doping asymmetry in cuprates; provides qualitative explanation for strange metal behavior, pseudogap phase, and underdoped isotope effect.
AI.conclusion: Hund's coupling J provides the essential attractive component within an otherwise repulsive U_eff = U - J interaction, enabling fractional charge binding and unconventional superconductivity. The model successfully captures multiple key features of cuprate superconductors though some aspects (pseudogap, isotope effect) remain qualitative.

title: Direct Imaging of Temperature Evolution of Polar Nanoregions and Chemically Ordered Regions in PMN Relaxor: Evidence for Polar Phase Percolation
summary: Polar nanoregions (PNRs) are central to understanding the exceptional dielectric and piezoelectric properties of relaxor ferroelectrics and are key to advancing dielectrics for high-energy storage. However, direct real-space imaging of their formation and evolution remains a major challenge in condensed matter physics. Here, we report the real-space mappings of both PNRs and chemically ordered regions (CORs) in the prototypical relaxor Pb(Mg1/3Nb2/3)O3 and their temperature dependence using convergent-beam electron diffraction (CBED) combined with four-dimensional scanning transmission electron microscopy (4D-STEM). The results reveal that CORs, with sizes of 2-5 nm, remain static with temperature and act to suppress PNR growth. In contrast, PNRs evolve from isolated 2-5 nm regions at room temperature to interconnected structures ~10 nm in size at low temperatures, indicative of a percolation transition. These observations support the random-field model, in which PNRs emerge from a paraelectric matrix and their growth and collective interactions are constrained by random local fields associated with CORs.

AI.tldr: This study uses CBED combined with 4D-STEM to directly image polar nanoregions (PNRs) and chemically ordered regions (CORs) in Pb(Mg1/3Nb2/3)O3 relaxor ferroelectric, revealing their temperature-dependent evolution and supporting the random-field model.
AI.motivation: Direct real-space imaging of PNRs and their formation/evolution has been a major challenge in condensed matter physics, despite PNRs being central to understanding exceptional dielectric and piezoelectric properties of relaxor ferroelectrics and advancing dielectrics for high-energy storage applications.
AI.method: Convergent-beam electron diffraction (CBED) combined with four-dimensional scanning transmission electron microscopy (4D-STEM) was used to map both PNRs and CORs in the prototypical relaxor Pb(Mg1/3Nb2/3)O3 and study their temperature dependence.
AI.result: CORs (2-5 nm) remain static with temperature and suppress PNR growth. PNRs evolve from isolated 2-5 nm regions at room temperature to interconnected structures ~10 nm at low temperatures, indicating a percolation transition.
AI.conclusion: The observations support the random-field model, where PNRs emerge from a paraelectric matrix with their growth and collective interactions constrained by random local fields associated with CORs.

title: Zero-energy bound state trapped in line-shaped vortex in topological superconductor
summary: Fermion bound states in the core of a line-shaped vortex of a two-dimensional topological superconductor are investigated. The superconducting pairing potential, described in terms of elliptical coordinates, vanishes along a line defect with the two foci at the endpoints. The superconductivity is induced into a topological insulator via proximity effect with a type II s-wave superconductor. The spin and the momentum are perpendicularly locked by the strong spin-orbit coupling via Rashba interaction. A zero-energy Majorana state arises from the Berry phase together with a sequence of equally spaced fermion exitations. By solving the Bogoliubov-de Gennes equations using the method employed by Caroli, de Gennes and Matricon we calculate the energies, the wave-functions and spin-polarization of the bound states. An analytic expression for the local density of states within the vortex is obtained.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates fermion bound states in a line-shaped vortex of a two-dimensional topological superconductor, where a zero-energy Majorana state emerges alongside a sequence of equally spaced fermion excitations due to Berry phase. The study uses the Bogoliubov-de Gennes equations with the Caroli-de Gennes-Matricon method to calculate energies, wavefunctions, spin-polarization, and obtains an analytic expression for the local density of states.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand Majorana zero modes in topological superconductors, which are crucial for topological quantum computing. The specific focus on a line-shaped vortex with elliptical geometry provides a novel platform for studying these exotic states.
AI.method: The researchers solved the Bogoliubov-de Gennes equations using the Caroli-de Gennes-Matricon method, describing the superconducting pairing potential in elliptical coordinates. They investigated a topological insulator with proximity-induced superconductivity from a type II s-wave superconductor, with strong Rashba spin-orbit coupling locking spin and momentum perpendicularly.
AI.result: The key results include the emergence of a zero-energy Majorana state arising from the Berry phase, along with a sequence of equally spaced fermion excitations. The team calculated the energies, wavefunctions, spin-polarization, and derived an analytic expression for the local density of states within the vortex.
AI.conclusion: The study advances the understanding of Majorana fermions in topological superconductors and provides analytical tools for studying bound states in elliptical vortex geometries, which could be valuable for future topological quantum computation applications.

title: Three-Dimensional Non-Foliated Fractional Quantum Hall Phases with Irrational Anyons in Twisted van der Waals Multilayers
summary: Three-dimensional fractional quantum Hall phases offer a route to intrinsically higher-dimensional topological order beyond simple stacks of two-dimensional quantum Hall liquids. Such phases can exhibit non-foliated, intrinsically three-dimensional entanglement structures, exponentially large topological degeneracies and quasiparticles with irrational braiding statistics. Their microscopic realization has remained elusive because Landau quantization in three dimensions generally leaves dispersive one-dimensional bands, favoring metallic and density-wave states over incompressible fractional liquids. Here we show that large-angle twisted van der Waals multilayers provide a practical route around this obstruction. Large twist angles suppress coherent interlayer tunneling through momentum mismatch, while the atomic-scale layer separation preserves strong interlayer Coulomb interactions. Using Monte Carlo calculations to compare the energies of an extensive set of 862 competing trial wavefunctions, we find that generalized Halperin states with quantum coherence extending across multiple consecutive layers are stabilized. In experimentally accessible magnetic-field regimes, these states replace the metallic spontaneous-interlayer-coherent phases that dominate conventional untwisted graphite-like multilayers. The resulting liquids realize non-foliated fractional quantum Hall order closely related to fractonic topological order, hosting quasiparticles with rational electric charges but irrational braiding statistics. Their large topological degeneracy and non-rational statistical phases may offer unconventional resources for quantum information storage and processing. Our results establish twisted van der Waals multilayers as a realistic materials platform for three-dimensional fractional Hall matter beyond conventional two-dimensional quantum Hall systems.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that twisted van der Waals multilayers provide a realistic platform for realizing three-dimensional fractional quantum Hall phases, which have been previously elusive due to complications in 3D Landau quantization. The researchers used Monte Carlo calculations to compare 862 competing trial wavefunctions and found that generalized Halperin states with multi-layer quantum coherence are stabilized at large twist angles, offering a new form of topological matter with irrational braiding statistics.
AI.motivation: The main motivation is to overcome the fundamental obstruction preventing the realization of 3D fractional quantum Hall phases. In conventional 3D systems, Landau quantization typically produces dispersive 1D bands that favor metallic and density-wave states rather than incompressible fractional liquids. The authors seek a practical materials platform that can host these exotic topological phases with their potentially useful properties for quantum information.
AI.method: The researchers employed Monte Carlo calculations to perform extensive energy comparisons among an extensive set of 862 competing trial wavefunctions. They studied twisted van der Waals multilayers at large angles, which suppress coherent interlayer tunneling through momentum mismatch while preserving strong interlayer Coulomb interactions due to atomic-scale layer separation.
AI.result: The calculations reveal that generalized Halperin states with quantum coherence extending across multiple consecutive layers become stabilized in these systems. In experimentally accessible magnetic field regimes, these states replace the metallic spontaneous-interlayer-coherent phases found in conventional untwisted graphite-like multilayers. The resulting liquids realize non-foliated fractional quantum Hall order related to fractonic topological order, hosting quasiparticles with rational electric charges but irrational braiding statistics.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes twisted van der Waals multilayers as a realistic materials platform for 3D fractional Hall matter beyond conventional 2D quantum Hall systems. These phases offer exponentially large topological degeneracies and non-rational statistical phases that may serve as unconventional resources for quantum information storage and processing.

title: Resistivity in Dilute Cu-3d Alloys Governed by Disorder-Induced Band Broadening
comment: 20 pages, 9 fiqures (main text); 10 pages, 4 figures (supplemental material)
summary: The mechanism governing the resistivity in dilute Cu-3d transition-metal alloys at ambient temperature -- the regime relevant to most practical applications and distinct from the low-temperature, Kondo-screened regime addressed by earlier theoretical work -- is investigated using first-principles calculations based on the Korringa--Kohn--Rostoker coherent potential approximation combined with the Kubo--Greenwood formalism. The paramagnetic state is described within the disordered local moment (DLM) framework, corresponding to a local-moment paramagnet rather than a Pauli paramagnet. We show that the experimentally observed resistivity trends are reproduced only within the DLM description, while nonmagnetic and ferromagnetic states fail to capture the correct element dependence. Contrary to conventional interpretations based on the density of states at the Fermi level, the resistivity exhibits a strong correlation with the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the Bloch spectral function (BSF) on the Fermi surface. This correlation reflects the disorder-induced lifetime broadening of electronic states, directly related to the scattering rate that governs electrical resistivity. A common power-law scaling between resistivity and BSF broadening is identified across different magnetic states. These results demonstrate that the resistivity is governed by disorder-induced band broadening in momentum space rather than by local density-of-states effects, providing a unified microscopic interpretation of the breakdown of Linde's rule in Cu-based alloys.

AI.tldr: First-principles study reveals that electrical resistivity in dilute Cu-3d transition-metal alloys at ambient temperature is governed by disorder-induced band broadening (lifetime broadening) in momentum space, not by local density-of-states effects. This provides a unified microscopic interpretation for the breakdown of Linde's rule.
AI.motivation: Previous theoretical work addressed the low-temperature, Kondo-screened regime, but the mechanism governing resistivity at ambient temperature (relevant to practical applications) was not well understood. Experimental resistivity trends needed to be explained from a fundamental perspective.
AI.method: First-principles calculations using Korringa--Kohn--Rostoker coherent potential approximation (KKR-CPA) combined with Kubo--Greenwood formalism. The paramagnetic state was described within the disordered local moment (DLM) framework.
AI.result: The experimentally observed resistivity trends are reproduced only within the DLM description, while nonmagnetic and ferromagnetic states fail. Resistivity correlates strongly with the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the Bloch spectral function (BSF) on the Fermi surface, not with the density of states at the Fermi level. A common power-law scaling between resistivity and BSF broadening was identified across different magnetic states.
AI.conclusion: The resistivity is governed by disorder-induced lifetime broadening of electronic states in momentum space rather than local density-of-states effects. This provides a unified microscopic interpretation of the breakdown of Linde's rule in Cu-based alloys.

title: Theoretical prediction of structural stability and superconductivity in T-hexagonal molybdenum dihydrides Monolayer
summary: The realization of ambient-pressure, high-temperature superconductivity in hydrogen-rich materials remains a major pursuit in condensed-matter physics. While bulk hydrides require extreme pressures to stabilize, two-dimensional (2D) transition-metal hydrides offer a promising alternative to bypass these compression constraints. In this work, we investigate the structural stability, electronic properties, and phonon-mediated superconductivity of a hexagonal molybdenum dihydride (MoH2) monolayer using first-principles calculations. Total-energy evaluations reveal that the octahedral T-phase is energetically more favorable than the previously reported trigonal prismatic H-phase by 0.198 eV, establishing the T-phase as the true ground-state configuration. Consequently, we systematically evaluate the lattice dynamics and superconducting properties of this ground-state T-MoH2 monolayer within the frameworks of density functional perturbation theory (DFPT) and the anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg formalism. The transition metal-hydrogen vibrational networks induce strong electron-phonon coupling (EPC), yielding an integrated coupling parameter of λ= 1.04. Solving the anisotropic Eliashberg equations predicts a conventional superconducting transition temperature (Tc of 14.4K) at ambient pressure, characterized by a moderately gap distribution (Δ= 2.07-3.01meV at 5.0 K). Our findings highlight the T-MoH2 monolayer as a structurally, mechanically, and thermally stable platform for exploring low-dimensional conventional superconductivity under ambient conditions.

AI.tldr: This study investigates a 2D hexagonal molybdenum dihydride (MoH2) monolayer for ambient-pressure, high-temperature superconductivity using first-principles calculations. The octahedral T-phase was found to be more stable than the previously reported H-phase by 0.198 eV. Strong electron-phonon coupling (λ=1.04) yields a superconducting transition temperature (Tc) of 14.4K at ambient pressure, making T-MoH2 a promising platform for low-dimensional conventional superconductivity.
AI.motivation: The pursuit of ambient-pressure, high-temperature superconductivity in hydrogen-rich materials is a major goal in condensed-matter physics. While bulk hydrides require extreme pressures, 2D transition-metal hydrides offer a promising alternative to bypass these compression constraints.
AI.method: First-principles calculations using density functional perturbation theory (DFPT) and the anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg formalism were employed to investigate structural stability, electronic properties, lattice dynamics, and phonon-mediated superconductivity of the MoH2 monolayer.
AI.result: The octahedral T-phase is energetically more favorable than the H-phase by 0.198 eV. Strong electron-phonon coupling (λ=1.04) was achieved. The predicted Tc is 14.4K at ambient pressure with a gap distribution of 2.07-3.01 meV at 5.0 K.
AI.conclusion: The T-MoH2 monolayer is structurally, mechanically, and thermally stable, serving as a promising platform for exploring low-dimensional conventional superconductivity under ambient conditions without requiring extreme pressures.

title: The Sample Pre-selection and Characterization Station at the SECUF: Instrumentation, Capabilities, and Representative Scientific Achievements
summary: The Synergetic Extreme Condition User Facility (SECUF) is a comprehensive, state-of-the-art user facility designed to provide integrated extreme physical conditions-including ultrahigh pressure, ultralow temperature, strong magnetic fields, and ultrafast optical fields-for frontier research in condensed matter physics and materials science. Within SECUF, the F2 Sample Pre-selection and Characterization Station plays a pivotal supporting role. Its mission is to provide comprehensive sample synthesis, processing, pre-screening, and characterization services to prepare high-quality specimens for subsequent experiments under extreme conditions. This paper details the specifications and performance of ten core instrument systems within these units. Furthermore, we highlight several breakthrough scientific achievements enabled by the F2 Station, encompassing the discovery of novel quantum spin supersolid states, pressure-induced high-temperature superconductivity in nickelates, giant anomalous Hall angles, and molecular water in lunar soil. We also outline ongoing technical developments that expand the station's capabilities, such as integrated high-pressure cells and self-built ancillary measurement systems.

AI.tldr: The SECUF F2 Station is a comprehensive sample preparation facility supporting extreme condition research in condensed matter physics and materials science, featuring ten core instrument systems for sample synthesis, processing, pre-screening, and characterization.
AI.motivation: To provide high-quality specimen preparation services for researchers conducting experiments under extreme physical conditions (ultrahigh pressure, ultralow temperature, strong magnetic fields, ultrafast optical fields) at the SECUF facility.
AI.method: The paper details the specifications and performance of ten core instrument systems within the F2 Station, including sample synthesis, processing, pre-screening, and characterization capabilities.
AI.result: The F2 Station has enabled breakthrough scientific discoveries including: novel quantum spin supersolid states, pressure-induced high-temperature superconductivity in nickelates, giant anomalous Hall angles, and molecular water in lunar soil. Technical developments continue with integrated high-pressure cells and self-built ancillary measurement systems.
AI.conclusion: The F2 Sample Pre-selection and Characterization Station serves as a critical supporting infrastructure for SECUF, successfully enabling frontier research in condensed matter physics and materials science through comprehensive sample preparation capabilities.

title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
summary: Accurate constitutive modeling of hot deformation behavior is essential for designing thermomechanical processes in advanced structural alloys. Conventional Arrhenius-type and empirical models do not adequately capture the combined effects of strain hardening, dynamic recovery (DRV), and dynamic recrystallization (DRX) across broad processing conditions. In this study, two Stacked Residual Physics-Informed Neural Networks (STAR-PINNs) were developed to simulate the hot deformation response of a Mo-rich $α+β$ titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4Mo-1V-0.1Si). The Enhanced STAR-PINN incorporated thermomechanical constitutive constraints, while the DRX-Aware STAR-PINN employed a dual-output architecture to account for recrystallization kinetics. Both models used a shared residual encoder trained on experimental flow stress data collected at temperatures from 800 to 1050 degrees C and strain rates between 0.01 and 10 per second. Physics-informed constraints, including thermal softening, strain-rate sensitivity, strain hardening, and post-peak softening, were enforced through automatic differentiation. The DRX-Aware model further integrated JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and Arrhenius-based consistency with tunable parameters, directly linking the predicted DRX fraction to stress output via latent-feature fusion. The DRX-Aware STAR-PINN achieved RMSE = 11.69 MPa, MAE = 4.83 MPa, R^2 = 0.9850, and a cross-validated RMSE of 12.47 +/- 0.26 MPa. This model accurately reproduced temperature-dependent flow curves, DRX kinetics, and Zener-Hollomon relationships, while maintaining physically consistent constitutive behavior. These results demonstrate that physics-informed deep learning provides a robust and interpretable framework for constitutive modeling, offering a practical approach for advanced process modeling of titanium alloys.

AI.tldr: This study develops Stacked Residual Physics-Informed Neural Networks (STAR-PINNs) for constitutive modeling of hot deformation in a Ti-6Al-4Mo-1V-0.1Si titanium alloy, achieving high accuracy (R² = 0.9850) across broad processing conditions.
AI.motivation: Conventional Arrhenius-type and empirical models fail to adequately capture combined effects of strain hardening, dynamic recovery (DRV), and dynamic recrystallization (DRX) across broad processing conditions, necessitating more advanced modeling approaches.
AI.method: Two STAR-PINN architectures were developed: (1) Enhanced STAR-PINN with thermomechanical constitutive constraints, and (2) DRX-Aware STAR-PINN with dual-output architecture incorporating JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and Arrhenius-based consistency. Both models used shared residual encoder trained on experimental flow stress data (800-1050°C, 0.01-10 s⁻¹) with physics-informed constraints enforced via automatic differentiation.
AI.result: DRX-Aware STAR-PINN achieved RMSE = 11.69 MPa, MAE = 4.83 MPa, R² = 0.9850, and cross-validated RMSE of 12.47 ± 0.26 MPa. The model accurately reproduced temperature-dependent flow curves, DRX kinetics, and Zener-Hollomon relationships while maintaining physically consistent constitutive behavior.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed deep learning provides a robust and interpretable framework for constitutive modeling of titanium alloys, offering a practical approach for advanced thermomechanical process design.

title: Direct observation of photon-induced vortices in superconducting films
comment: Submitted to Physical Review Applied. Presented at SPW 2026
summary: Nucleation of vortex-antivortex pairs (VAPs) is believed to play a central role in the photon detection mechanism of superconducting detectors; however, their direct dynamic observation has remained challenging. Here, we report the direct observation of photon-induced VAP dynamics in a current-carrying superconductor as quantized voltage signals following photon absorption. The observed signals are interpreted as discrete phase-slip events, where each vortex traversal induces a 2-pi phase change of the superconducting order parameter, resulting in a quantized voltage pulse whose time integral is given by the magnetic flux quantum. We analyze the resulting quantized signals as a function of bias current, base temperature, and input photon-number states, and find that the number of VAPs generated per absorbed photon becomes effectively stabilized under specific conditions. Under these conditions, we demonstrate photon-number-resolving capability by directly counting phase-slip-induced voltage quanta. Our results reveal a detection mechanism governed by phase dynamics rather than conventional resistive transitions. We further show that photon-number resolution emerges when the fluctuation of photon-induced vortex-antivortex pair generation becomes statistically suppressed. These findings establish a new route toward photon-number-resolving detection based on phase-slip counting and open opportunities for high-speed superconducting detectors for quantum optics and photonic quantum technologies.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first direct observation of photon-induced vortex-antivortex pair (VAP) dynamics in a current-carrying superconductor, demonstrating that photon absorption generates quantized voltage signals corresponding to phase-slip events. The work shows photon-number-resolving capability under specific conditions where VAP generation becomes stabilized.
AI.motivation: Nucleation of vortex-antivortex pairs is believed to be central to photon detection in superconducting detectors, but direct dynamic observation has remained challenging. The authors aimed to directly observe these VAP dynamics to understand the photon detection mechanism.
AI.method: The researchers observed quantized voltage signals following photon absorption in a current-carrying superconductor. These signals were interpreted as discrete phase-slip events where each vortex traversal induces a 2π phase change, resulting in quantized voltage pulses with time integral equal to the magnetic flux quantum. They analyzed signals as a function of bias current, base temperature, and input photon-number states.
AI.result: The number of VAPs generated per absorbed photon becomes effectively stabilized under specific conditions. Under these conditions, photon-number-resolving capability was demonstrated by directly counting phase-slip-induced voltage quanta. Photon-number resolution emerges when fluctuation of photon-induced VAP generation becomes statistically suppressed.
AI.conclusion: The findings establish a new detection mechanism governed by phase dynamics rather than conventional resistive transitions. This establishes a new route toward photon-number-resolving detection based on phase-slip counting, opening opportunities for high-speed superconducting detectors for quantum optics and photonic quantum technologies.

title: Dirac topology, anomalous Hall response, and giant magnetoresistance in carrier-compensated altermagnetic semimetal NiS
comment: Accepted in Phys. Rev. B Letter
summary: We combine first-principles density-functional theory, Berry-curvature analysis, semiclassical Boltzmann transport, and atomistic spin dynamics to establish hexagonal NiS as a compensated 3d altermagnetic semimetal in which topology, magnetism, and lattice dynamics are intrinsically intertwined. The rotational coset symmetry of the NiAs lattice produces momentum-dependent spin splitting characteristic of altermagnetism. With spin-orbit coupling, gapped Dirac-like crossings generate intense Berry-curvature hot spots and nearly compensated electron-hole pockets. This leads to a large and anisotropic intrinsic spin Hall conductivity comparable to that of several 4d, 5d metals, a symmetry-allowed anomalous Hall response despite zero net magnetization, and nonsaturating magnetoresistance exceeding 10000 percent. On the magnetic side, first-principles determination of the exchange tensor reveals dominant long-range superexchange and sizable anisotropic interactions, quantitatively reproducing the experimental Neel temperature. Our results identify NiS as a model 3d platform in which carrier compensation, altermagnetic symmetry, Berry-curvature driven transport, and lattice-sensitive magnetism coexist within a single symmetry framework, offering a design principle for multi-functional quantum responses in correlated transition-metal compounds.

AI.tldr: Hexagonal NiS is identified as a compensated 3d altermagnetic semimetal combining topology, magnetism, and lattice dynamics. The material exhibits large spin Hall conductivity, anomalous Hall response despite zero net magnetization, and magnetoresistance exceeding 10000%. This represents a model 3d platform for multi-functional quantum responses.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to identify a 3d transition-metal compound that combines carrier compensation, altermagnetic symmetry, Berry-curvature driven transport, and lattice-sensitive magnetism within a single symmetry framework, serving as a model platform for studying these intertwined phenomena.
AI.method: The methods combined include: first-principles density-functional theory, Berry-curvature analysis, semiclassical Boltzmann transport, and atomistic spin dynamics.
AI.result: Key results include: (1) Rotational coset symmetry produces momentum-dependent spin splitting characteristic of altermagnetism; (2) Gapped Dirac-like crossings generate Berry-curvature hot spots and nearly compensated electron-hole pockets; (3) Large anisotropic intrinsic spin Hall conductivity comparable to 4d/5d metals; (4) Symmetry-allowed anomalous Hall response despite zero net magnetization; (5) Nonsaturating magnetoresistance >10000%; (6) Dominant long-range superexchange and sizable anisotropic interactions reproducing experimental Neel temperature.
AI.conclusion: NiS is established as a model 3d platform for altermagnetic materials where compensated carrier pockets, topological Berry curvature, and lattice-sensitive magnetism coexist, offering a design principle for multi-functional quantum responses in correlated transition-metal compounds.

title: Hybridization-controlled re-entrant electronic phase switching and moire-confined states in twisted bilayer PtTe2
summary: Twisting a van der Waals bilayer changes not only the moiré periodicity but also the local stacking and interlayer hybridization. Here, we show, using fully relaxed first-principles calculations including spin--orbit coupling, band unfolding, and Brillouin-zone-integrated densities of states, that bilayer PtTe$_2$ exhibits a non-monotonic evolution between gapless and gapped electronic regimes. The $7.34^\circ$ structure remains gapless, whereas finite direct gaps appear at the sampled intermediate angles. The gap closes at the sampled $60^\circ$ configuration and reopens at higher angles. The direct gap shows an overall increase with the minimum local interlayer Pt--Pt separation, although the complete distribution of local stacking environments is required to account for deviations from this trend. At $7.34^\circ$, the low-energy states are concentrated predominantly in the AA-like regions of the otherwise gapless moiré cell. Controlled interlayer-separation scans show that increasing the layer spacing removes the near-$E_F$ crossings and opens a gap, consistent with weakened interlayer Te-$p_z$ hybridization. These results identify the redistribution of interlayer hybridization as the microscopic origin of the re-entrant gap evolution in twisted bilayer PtTe$_2$.

AI.tldr: Twisted bilayer PtTe2 exhibits a non-monotonic gap evolution with twist angle, transitioning between gapless and gapped states. Using first-principles calculations, researchers found that 7.34° structure remains gapless, intermediate angles show finite direct gaps, gap closes at 60°, and reopens at higher angles. The mechanism is attributed to redistribution of interlayer Te-pz hybridization controlled by local stacking and interlayer separation.
AI.motivation: Understand how twist angle affects electronic properties in twisted van der Waals bilayers, specifically exploring the relationship between moiré periodicity, local stacking, interlayer hybridization, and gap evolution in bilayer PtTe2.
AI.method: Fully relaxed first-principles calculations including spin-orbit coupling, band unfolding, and Brillouin-zone-integrated densities of states, with controlled interlayer-separation scans.
AI.result: The gap evolution is non-monotonic: gapless at 7.34°, finite direct gaps at intermediate angles, gap closes at 60°, reopens at higher angles. Direct gap increases with minimum local interlayer Pt-Pt separation. At 7.34°, low-energy states concentrate in AA-like regions. Increasing layer spacing opens a gap by weakening interlayer Te-pz hybridization.
AI.conclusion: Redistribution of interlayer hybridization is the microscopic origin of the re-entrant gap evolution in twisted bilayer PtTe2. The complete distribution of local stacking environments is needed to account for deviations from the trend between gap and interlayer separation.

title: Superconducting proximity effect in a strongly correlated charge-transfer insulator
summary: Proximity-induced superconductivity in strongly correlated insulators provides a versatile route for engineering quantum states of matter and artificial systems with tailored functionalities. However, microscopic interplay between superconductivity and correlated insulating states remains poorly understood. Here we use ultralow-temperature scanning tunnelling microscopy (STM) to systemically investigate superconducting proximity effects in a charge-transfer insulator. Via STM tip manipulation, atomically sharp lateral junctions composed of superconducting monolayer H-NbSe2 and charge-transfer insulating monolayer T-NbSe2 are constructed, enabling direct access to tunable coupling regimes. In the weak-coupling regime, there is a robust proximity-induced superconducting gap in T-NbSe2, with a reduced gap value relative to that of H-NbSe2. Upon entering the strong-coupling regime, T-NbSe2 exhibits a superconducting gap comparable to that of H-NbSe2, accompanied by pronounced particle-hole-symmetric in-gap bound states, consistent with Yu-Shiba-Rusinov-like excitations. These findings establish monolayer H/T-NbSe2 lateral junctions as a model platform for elucidating superconducting proximity effects in strongly correlated charge-transfer insulators.

AI.tldr: This study investigates superconducting proximity effects in strongly correlated charge-transfer insulators using ultralow-temperature STM. By constructing atomically sharp lateral junctions between superconducting H-NbSe2 and insulating T-NbSe2 monolayers, researchers observed distinct behavior in weak and strong coupling regimes, including proximity-induced superconducting gaps and Yu-Shiba-Rusinov-like excitations.
AI.motivation: The microscopic interplay between superconductivity and correlated insulating states remains poorly understood. This work aims to establish a model platform for elucidating these proximity effects in strongly correlated charge-transfer insulators.
AI.method: Ultralow-temperature scanning tunnelling microscopy (STM) was used with STM tip manipulation to construct atomically sharp lateral junctions between superconducting monolayer H-NbSe2 and charge-transfer insulating monolayer T-NbSe2, enabling direct access to tunable coupling regimes.
AI.result: In the weak-coupling regime, a robust proximity-induced superconducting gap appears in T-NbSe2 with reduced gap value. In the strong-coupling regime, T-NbSe2 exhibits a superconducting gap comparable to H-NbSe2, accompanied by pronounced particle-hole-symmetric in-gap bound states consistent with Yu-Shiba-Rusinov-like excitations.
AI.conclusion: Monolayer H/T-NbSe2 lateral junctions serve as a model platform for understanding superconducting proximity effects in strongly correlated charge-transfer insulators, providing a versatile route for engineering quantum states of matter.

title: Hubbard-assisted stability of Hatsugai-Kohmoto correlations in a one-dimensional open chain
summary: The Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) model has recently been identified as a fixed-point description of Mottness that is stable against perturbing local interactions. This raises a natural question beyond the weak-perturbation regime: how does a finite local repulsion modify HK physics in real-space observables and local spectra? We address this question in a half-filled one-dimensional spinful fermion chain, where the HK interaction is supplemented by an onsite Hubbard repulsion. Using exact diagonalization in an open chain, we compute ground-state correlation functions and site-resolved local spectra. We find that the Hubbard term does not drive the system away from the HK-dominated regime. Instead, at moderate $U_{\rm HK}$, a finite $U_{\rm Hub}$ promotes the emergence of behavior characteristic of the pure HK chain at larger $U_{\rm HK}$. This Hubbard-assisted strong-HK response is reflected consistently in the development of positive spin correlations, the suppression of single-particle coherence, the impurity-induced redistribution of local spectral weight, and the evolution of pairing correlations. As a control perturbation, we replace the onsite Hubbard interaction by a nearest-neighbor (NN) density interaction. In contrast to the Hubbard case, the HK and NN interactions display competing tendencies and do not reproduce the same strong-HK behavior. These results provide a nonperturbative real-space complement to the fixed-point stability of HK physics and show that the effect of an additional repulsive interaction depends sensitively on its spatial structure.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how finite onsite Hubbard repulsion modifies the Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) model physics in a half-filled 1D spinful fermion chain. Using exact diagonalization, they find that a finite Hubbard interaction actually promotes strong-HK behavior rather than competing with it, as reflected in spin correlations, spectral weight redistribution, and pairing correlations. In contrast, nearest-neighbor density interaction competes with HK physics.
AI.motivation: The HK model has been identified as a fixed-point description of Mottness stable against local perturbations. The authors seek to understand how finite local repulsion modifies HK physics in real-space observables and local spectra beyond the weak-perturbation regime.
AI.method: Exact diagonalization in an open half-filled 1D spinful fermion chain, supplemented by onsite Hubbard repulsion. They computed ground-state correlation functions and site-resolved local spectra as observables.
AI.result: The Hubbard term does not drive the system away from the HK-dominated regime. At moderate UKH, a finite U_Hub promotes behavior characteristic of pure HK chain at larger U_HK. This is reflected in positive spin correlations, suppression of single-particle coherence, impurity-induced spectral weight redistribution, and evolution of pairing correlations. In contrast, nearest-neighbor density interaction competes with HK and does not reproduce strong-HK behavior.
AI.conclusion: These results provide nonperturbative real-space evidence for the fixed-point stability of HK physics and demonstrate that the effect of additional repulsive interaction depends sensitively on its spatial structure - Hubbard (onsite) enhances HK behavior while NN (nearest-neighbor) competes with it.

title: Rotation topological states: theory and material realization
summary: The conventional characterization of topological materials relies on topological invariants calculated from the entire set of occupied bands. However, when a system possesses rotational symmetry, the occupied Hilbert space can be decomposed into multiple subspaces labeled by distinct rotation eigenvalues. We show that this decomposition reveals hidden topological states characterized by a novel $\mathbb{Z}_2^n$ topological invariant, where $n$ is the number of subspaces, while the conventional $\mathbb{Z}_2$ invariant may fail to detect the topology hidden in the rotation subspaces. Remarkably, time-reversal symmetry pairs conjugate rotation eigenvalues and guarantees that the two subspaces have the same $\mathbb{Z}_2$ invariants, making the topology always hidden from the conventional global invariant. We formulate the theory of rotation-subspace topology and demonstrate its material realization in bulk CsCl. Using first-principles calculations and symmetry analysis, we show that bulk CsCl, which is diagnosed as topologically trivial by the conventional approach, features a nontrivial $\mathbb{Z}_2^3$ invariant along the $Γ$-R path and a nontrivial $\mathbb{Z}_2^4$ invariant along the $Γ$-Z and M-R paths, leading to double Weyl points on the (111) and (001) surfaces, respectively. The subspace $\mathbb{Z}_2^n$ invariant proposed here serves as a necessary refinement for symmetry-protected topological phases and will facilitate the identification of a large class of topological states overlooked by existing diagnostics.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals hidden topological states in materials with rotational symmetry by decomposing the occupied Hilbert space into rotation eigenvalue subspaces, introducing Z_2^n topological invariants that can detect topology invisible to conventional global Z_2 invariants. Bulk CsCl, previously considered topologically trivial, is shown to host nontrivial topology.
AI.motivation: Conventional topological characterization uses topological invariants calculated from the entire set of occupied bands, which may fail to detect topology hidden within rotation subspaces. Time-reversal symmetry pairs conjugate rotation eigenvalues, guaranteeing equal Z_2 invariants in subspaces and making this topology always hidden from conventional approaches.
AI.method: The authors formulated the theory of rotation-subspace topology and applied first-principles calculations combined with symmetry analysis to study bulk CsCl, examining different high-symmetry paths (Γ-R, Γ-Z, M-R) and surface states.
AI.result: Bulk CsCl, diagnosed as topologically trivial by conventional methods, features a nontrivial Z_2^3 invariant along the Γ-R path and a nontrivial Z_2^4 invariant along the Γ-Z and M-R paths. These hidden topological phases produce double Weyl points on the (111) and (001) surfaces, respectively.
AI.conclusion: The subspace Z_2^n invariant serves as a necessary refinement for symmetry-protected topological phases and will enable identification of a large class of topological states previously overlooked by existing diagnostic methods.

title: Quantum-classical crossover in finite spin-1/2 rings with Dzyaloshinsky-Moriya interaction
summary: We study finite spin-$\tfrac12$ rings with nearest-neighbor Heisenberg exchange, Dzyaloshinsky--Moriya interaction, and an external magnetic field. We introduce an interpolation parameter between the fully quantum Hamiltonian and a state-dependent mean-field description. Using dissipative Gisin--Schrödinger dynamics, we analyze the resulting quantum--classical crossover through local magnetization, connected spin correlations, single-site entropy, and the saturation field of the fully polarized state.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates finite spin-1/2 rings with Heisenberg exchange, Dzyaloshinsky-Moriya interaction, and external magnetic field, analyzing the quantum-to-classical crossover using an interpolation method between quantum and mean-field descriptions, examined through local magnetization, spin correlations, entropy, and saturation field.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand and analyze the quantum-classical crossover in finite quantum spin systems, which is a fundamental problem in quantum magnetism and mesoscopic physics. The study aims to bridge fully quantum mechanical descriptions with classical mean-field approximations.
AI.method: The method involves introducing an interpolation parameter that continuously connects the fully quantum Hamiltonian to a state-dependent mean-field description. The analysis uses dissipative Gisin-Schrödinger dynamics to study the crossover behavior.
AI.result: The results are obtained through analysis of local magnetization, connected spin correlations, single-site entropy, and the saturation field of the fully polarized state - these quantities characterize the quantum vs. classical behavior.
AI.conclusion: The study provides insights into the quantum-classical transition in finite spin systems, demonstrating how quantum coherence and entanglement effects diminish as the system approaches the classical limit through the interpolation scheme.

title: Research progress in high-pressure tuning of layered magnetic materials
summary: Two-dimensional van der Waals materials have enormous potential applications in many fields due to their unique layered structure and excellent properties. Compared with three-dimensional bulk materials, layered systems are coupled by weak van der Waals interactions between layers, endowing them with much higher structural compressibility, particularly along the interlayer direction, which is more sensitive to external pressure. High pressure can expand the accessible phase space, enabling the synthesis of new materials or the retention of metastable phases. On the microscopic level, pressure can significantly tune the interlayer structure and interactions, induce changes in the electronic structure, and consequently give rise to a variety of rich physical properties. This article systematically introduces in situ high-pressure experimental approaches, including diamond anvil cells combined with X-ray and spectroscopic techniques, high-pressure magnetic transport measurements, and emerging NV-center quantum sensing. It further reviews representative pressure-induced phenomena and underlying tuning mechanisms in layered magnetic materials, such as high-spin to low-spin transitions of transition-metal ions and the accompanying structural phase transitions and superconductivity; substantial enhancement of the Curie temperature and continuous switching of magnetocrystalline anisotropy; and antiferromagnetic-to-ferromagnetic transitions achieved by modulating exchange interactions or via stacking engineering. Finally, we discuss future directions, including synergistic multi-field control by combining pressure with electric fields and twisted heterostructures, as well as strategies such as pressure quenching to retain high-pressure metastable magnetic phases at ambient conditions.

AI.tldr: This review article comprehensively covers high-pressure research on 2D van der Waals materials, discussing experimental techniques, pressure-induced phenomena in layered magnetic materials, and future directions for controlling magnetic properties through pressure engineering.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional van der Waals materials have great application potential due to their unique layered structure and excellent properties. High pressure can expand accessible phase space for synthesizing new materials or retaining metastable phases, and significantly tune interlayer structure, interactions, and electronic structure to induce rich physical properties.
AI.method: The paper systematically introduces in situ high-pressure experimental approaches including: (1) diamond anvil cells combined with X-ray and spectroscopic techniques; (2) high-pressure magnetic transport measurements; (3) emerging NV-center quantum sensing.
AI.result: Representative pressure-induced phenomena in layered magnetic materials include: high-spin to low-spin transitions of transition-metal ions with accompanying structural phase transitions and superconductivity; substantial enhancement of Curie temperature and continuous switching of magnetocrystalline anisotropy; antiferromagnetic-to-ferromagnetic transitions achieved by modulating exchange interactions or via stacking engineering.
AI.conclusion: Future research directions include synergistic multi-field control by combining pressure with electric fields and twisted heterostructures, as well as strategies such as pressure quenching to retain high-pressure metastable magnetic phases at ambient conditions.

title: Transverse order and longitudinal fluctuations in a near-Ising spin supersolid
summary: We investigate the polarization of the ordered moments and low-energy spin fluctuations in the spin supersolid state of the S = 1/2 triangular-lattice easy-axis antiferromagnet K2Co(SeO3)2 using neutron scattering. The supersolid order develops through successive BKT transitions: the longitudinal order appears at higher temperature, while the transverse component associated with the Bose-Einstein condensate emerges only below a lower-temperature transition accompanied by a change in the interlayer correlations. At the lowest measured temperature, the transverse ordered moment reaches only 11% of the longitudinal component. Moreover, the low-energy spin fluctuations are found to be predominantly longitudinal in character. These results provide direct evidence that the supersolid ground state survives even in the near-Ising regime and exhibits longitudinal low-energy dynamics, imposing stringent constraints on microscopic theories of triangular-lattice XXZ antiferromagnets.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the spin supersolid state in the S=1/2 triangular-lattice easy-axis antiferromagnet K2Co(SeO3)2 using neutron scattering, revealing that the supersolid ground state survives in the near-Ising regime with predominantly longitudinal low-energy dynamics.
AI.motivation: To investigate the polarization of ordered moments and low-energy spin fluctuations in the spin supersolid state, and to determine whether the supersolid ground state can survive in the near-Ising regime of triangular-lattice XXZ antiferromagnets.
AI.method: Neutron scattering experiments were conducted on K2Co(SeO3)2 to study the spin supersolid state.
AI.result: The supersolid order develops through successive BKT transitions: longitudinal order appears at higher temperature, while transverse component (associated with Bose-Einstein condensation) emerges only below a lower-temperature transition. At the lowest temperature, the transverse ordered moment reaches only 11% of the longitudinal component, and low-energy spin fluctuations are predominantly longitudinal in character.
AI.conclusion: These results provide direct evidence that the supersolid ground state survives even in the near-Ising regime and exhibits longitudinal low-energy dynamics, imposing stringent constraints on microscopic theories of triangular-lattice XXZ antiferromagnets.

title: Role of small-radius and high-electronegativity A-Site dopants in enhancing proton transport and stability of perovskite electrolytes
comment: 15 pages and 11+4 figures. The version that accepted for publication in Phys. Rev. B
summary: The practical application of BaCeO$_3$-based electrolytes is limited by their poor chemical stability in proton-conducting solid oxide fuel cells. Commonly employed B-site doping strategies typically improve proton transport with limited improvement in stability. Recent experiments show that A-site Ca doping can simultaneously enhance both properties. Here, through first-principles calculations and mechanistic analysis of Ca-doped BaCeO$_3$, we identify the synergistic roles of small-radius, high-electronegativity A-site dopants in governing proton transport and chemical stability in perovskite electrolytes. We show that the higher electronegativity of A-site dopant weakens the A-O ionic bonding, facilitating oxygen-vacancy formation and enhancing proton uptake by increasing the basicity. This weakened A-O interaction also suppresses the formation of impurity phases and reduces the adsorption strength of acidic gases such as CO$_2$ and SO$_2$. The lattice contraction induced by the smaller ionic radius improves thermal stability and can enhance proton diffusion in systems where proton transfer is the rate-limiting step. Furthermore, we find that Ca surface segregation can mitigate grain-boundary resistance effects. Our results demonstrate the advantages of A-site Ca doping in Ba-based electrolytes, clarify the mechanisms by which small-radius, high-electronegativity dopants influence proton transport and chemical stability, and provide guidance for the design of high-performance proton-conducting electrolytes.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates A-site Ca doping in BaCeO3 perovskite electrolytes for proton-conducting solid oxide fuel cells. Through first-principles calculations, researchers found that small-radius, high-electronegativity A-site dopants (like Ca) can simultaneously enhance both proton transport and chemical stability - addressing a key limitation of conventional B-site doping strategies.
AI.motivation: BaCeO3-based electrolytes have practical applications limited by poor chemical stability in proton-conducting solid oxide fuel cells. While B-site doping improves proton transport, it offers limited improvement in stability. The researchers sought to understand why A-site Ca doping can simultaneously enhance both properties.
AI.method: First-principles calculations and mechanistic analysis of Ca-doped BaCeO3
AI.result: Key mechanisms identified: (1) Higher electronegativity of A-site dopant weakens A-O ionic bonding, facilitating oxygen-vacancy formation; (2) Enhanced proton uptake by increasing basicity; (3) Suppressed impurity phase formation; (4) Reduced adsorption of acidic gases (CO2, SO2); (5) Lattice contraction from smaller ionic radius improves thermal stability; (6) Enhanced proton diffusion when proton transfer is rate-limiting; (7) Ca surface segregation mitigates grain-boundary resistance.
AI.conclusion: A-site Ca doping provides significant advantages for Ba-based electrolytes. Small-radius, high-electronegativity dopants offer a promising design strategy for high-performance proton-conducting electrolytes by simultaneously addressing proton transport and chemical stability limitations.

title: Stoner transitions beyond mean-field in two-dimensional electronic systems: a diagrammatic Monte Carlo study
summary: Stoner instabilities for fermions with repulsive interaction have been well studied within the mean-field (ladder) approximation. In this study, we consider two-dimensional electronic systems with one or two valleys and discuss a Stoner transition beyond the ladder approximation. At weak coupling, the corrections to the ladder approximation come predominantly from the renormalization of the particle-hole vertex in the particle-particle channel, and the lowest-order corrections are logarithmically singular in the low-density limit. To investigate the problem beyond the lowest order, we apply the diagrammatic Monte Carlo algorithm and treat ladder and non-ladder renormalizations on equal footing. We find that in a one-valley system, a Stoner transition to a ferromagnetism occurs at low density only if there is a cutoff on the momentum transfer carried by the interaction. In a two-valley system, the restriction is less severe. Here we find either a direct Stoner transition into a spin- and valley-polarized state, or a set of two Stoner transitions via an intermediate valley-polarized state. The pathway is controlled by the anisotropy of the fermionic dispersion.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates Stoner ferromagnetic instabilities in 2D electronic systems beyond the standard ladder approximation, using diagrammatic Monte Carlo methods. For one-valley systems, ferromagnetism requires a momentum cutoff; two-valley systems allow either direct or two-step transitions depending on dispersion anisotropy.
AI.motivation: The standard mean-field ladder approximation may not accurately predict Stoner transitions in real 2D materials, particularly at low densities where logarithmic singularities appear.
AI.method: Diagrammatic Monte Carlo algorithm treating ladder and non-ladder renormalizations on equal footing, focusing on particle-hole vertex corrections in the particle-particle channel.
AI.result: One-valley systems require a momentum-transfer cutoff for ferromagnetism; two-valley systems show either direct or two-step Stoner transitions controlled by dispersion anisotropy.
AI.conclusion: Ladder approximations alone are insufficient for predicting ferromagnetic instabilities in 2D systems; non-ladder corrections and valley degrees of freedom significantly alter the phase diagram.

title: Mapping recrystallization trajectories in GaAs using latent space diffraction analysis
summary:
Recrystallization in disordered solids proceeds through a sequence of local structural rearrangements that are difficult to resolve using conventional diffraction analysis. In amorphous and partially ordered materials, subtle variations in diffuse scattering, short-range order, and defect-mediated symmetry emergence encode the pathways through which ordering initiates and propagates. Here, we introduce a latent space framework for mapping these pathways directly from \textit{in situ} 4D-STEM diffraction data. A convolutional autoencoder provides a compact representation of structural motifs, and unsupervised clustering identifies recurring microstructural states, including amorphous, paracrystalline, crystalline, twinned, and hybrid intermediates. By tracking these states across temperature, we construct phase trajectory models that reveal the topology of the recrystallization landscape, including metastable basins, branching pathways, hybrid states, and temperature-dependent reorganizations of accessible states.
  Applied to ion irradiated GaAs, this approach uncovers two distinct recrystallization regimes separated by a transition near 250\textdegree{}C. At low temperature, recrystallization is growth-dominated and dominated by the persistence of amorphous and crystalline states. At high temperature, the transformation landscape reorganizes: hybrid and faulted states become metastable precursors to twinning, polycrystalline regions stabilize, and twinned structures emerge as dominant end states. The latent space representation also identifies amorphous patterns with weak symmetry signatures that precede recrystallization. This reveals structural precursors to ordering that are not captured by conventional descriptors give new insights into how recrystallization is initiated.

AI.tldr: A latent space framework using convolutional autoencoder and unsupervised clustering maps recrystallization pathways from in situ 4D-STEM diffraction data, revealing two distinct recrystallization regimes in ion-irradiated GaAs separated by a 250°C transition.
AI.motivation: Conventional diffraction analysis cannot resolve local structural rearrangements during recrystallization in disordered solids. The authors aim to decode subtle variations in diffuse scattering, short-range order, and defect-mediated symmetry to understand how ordering initiates and propagates.
AI.method: 4D-STEM diffraction data was analyzed using a convolutional autoencoder for compact representation of structural motifs, followed by unsupervised clustering to identify recurring microstructural states (amorphous, paracrystalline, crystalline, twinned, and hybrid intermediates). Phase trajectory models were constructed by tracking these states across temperature.
AI.result: Two distinct recrystallization regimes were identified in ion-irradiated GaAs: (1) Low temperature regime - growth-dominated with persistence of amorphous and crystalline states; (2) High temperature regime (>250°C) - landscape reorganizes with hybrid and faulted states becoming metastable precursors to twinning, polycrystalline regions stabilizing, and twinned structures emerging as dominant end states. Amorphous patterns with weak symmetry signatures were detected as precursors to recrystallization.
AI.conclusion: The latent space framework successfully reveals the topology of the recrystallization landscape including metastable basins, branching pathways, hybrid states, and temperature-dependent reorganizations. This approach captures structural precursors to ordering that conventional descriptors miss, providing new insights into recrystallization initiation mechanisms.

title: Magnetic quadrupole current generation and accumulation in noncentrosymmetric systems
summary: Magnetization control via magnetic octupole injection has recently been proposed for a new class of centrosymmetric antiferromagnets, namely $d$-wave altermagnets, where the magnetic octupole is the lowest-rank magnetic multipole allowed by symmetry and serves as an alternative carrier to spin injection. In contrast, in noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets, the magnetic quadrupole (MQ) constitutes the lowest-rank symmetry-allowed magnetic multipole, suggesting that MQ currents can provide an efficient route toward magnetization control through MQ injection. Here, we establish the symmetry conditions for MQ-current generation by constructing the multipole representation of the MQ conductivity tensor and show that MQ currents are generically allowed in noncentrosymmetric crystallographic point groups. As a representative example, we demonstrate MQ-current generation in the linear-response regime associated with symmetry lowering from the centrosymmetric point group ($mmm$) to its noncentrosymmetric subgroup ($mm2$). Furthermore, we reveal MQ accumulation near sample edges, analogous to spin accumulation induced by the spin Hall effect. This edge accumulation provides direct evidence of MQ-current generation and constitutes a key prerequisite for realizing MQ injection and MQ-based magnetization control in noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes symmetry conditions for magnetic quadrupole (MQ) current generation in noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets, demonstrating that MQ currents are allowed in noncentrosymmetric crystallographic point groups. The study reveals MQ accumulation near sample edges, analogous to spin Hall effect, providing a pathway for MQ-based magnetization control.
AI.motivation: While magnetic octupole injection has been proposed for centrosymmetric d-wave altermagnets, noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets have magnetic quadrupole as the lowest-rank symmetry-allowed magnetic multipole. This study aims to establish symmetry conditions for MQ-current generation as an efficient route toward magnetization control in noncentrosymmetric systems.
AI.method: The authors constructed the multipole representation of the MQ conductivity tensor to establish symmetry conditions for MQ-current generation. They used a representative example demonstrating MQ-current generation in the linear-response regime associated with symmetry lowering from the centrosymmetric point group (mmm) to its noncentrosymmetric subgroup (mm2).
AI.result: MQ currents are generically allowed in noncentrosymmetric crystallographic point groups. MQ accumulation occurs near sample edges, analogous to spin accumulation induced by the spin Hall effect. This edge accumulation provides direct evidence of MQ-current generation.
AI.conclusion: The edge MQ accumulation constitutes a key prerequisite for realizing MQ injection and MQ-based magnetization control in noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets, offering an alternative approach to magnetization control compared to magnetic octupole injection in centrosymmetric systems.

title: First-principles Study of Structural and Electronic Properties of Mn-doped Cu2NiXY4 (X=Sn, Ge, Si; Y=S, Se) Chalcogenide Semiconductors
summary: In this work, the effect of partial substitution of Mn by Ni on the structural and electronic properties of kesterite systems Cu2NiXY4 (X = Sn, Ge, Si; Y = S, Se) was studied using density functional theory (DFT). The mBJ+U method was used to characterize the bandgap more accurately. To the best of our knowledge, a systematic comparative study of Mn substitution in the entire Cu2NiXY4 (X = Sn, Ge, Si; Y = S, Se) family has not been previously performed. Due to crystallographic constraints of the kesterite unit cell, the substitution of a single Ni atom with Mn corresponds to 50%. This configuration was chosen to study the effect of Mn substitution on the structural and electronic properties of Cu2NiXY4 (X = Sn, Ge, Si; Y = S, Se) compounds, whereas the study of lower concentrations requires the use of a supercell and is the subject of further research. The calculation results show that the partial substitution of Ni with Mn preserves the tetragonal structure of kesterite and significantly alters the electronic structure. In all the compounds studied, the bandgap decreases from 1.028-3.397 to 1.007-3.333 eV. For example, in the Cu2NiSnS4 system, the bandgap width decreases from 1.59 eV to 1.49 eV. The narrowing of the bandgap results from hybridization between the Mn-3d, Cu-3d, and S/Se-p orbitals near the band edges, leading to a redistribution of electronic states around the Fermi level. The results demonstrate that Mn substitution is an effective strategy for controlling the electronic properties of Cu2NiXY4 kesterites, offering great promise for use in optoelectronic devices where adjustable bandgaps are required.

AI.tldr: This study uses DFT to investigate the effect of 50% Mn substitution for Ni on the structural and electronic properties of Cu2NiXY4 kesterite compounds (X = Sn, Ge, Si; Y = S, Se). The partial substitution preserves the tetragonal structure while narrowing the bandgap through orbital hybridization, demonstrating potential for optoelectronic applications.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how Mn doping affects the electronic properties of kesterite compounds, which are promising for optoelectronic devices. The study aims to provide a systematic comparative analysis of Mn substitution across the entire Cu2NiXY4 family, which had not been previously performed.
AI.method: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations with the mBJ+U method for accurate bandgap characterization. A 50% substitution level was studied due to crystallographic constraints of the kesterite unit cell.
AI.result: The partial substitution of Ni with Mn preserves the tetragonal kesterite structure while significantly altering electronic properties. Bandgap decreases from 1.028-3.397 eV to 1.007-3.333 eV across all compounds. For Cu2NiSnS4, the bandgap decreases from 1.59 eV to 1.49 eV. The bandgap narrowing results from hybridization between Mn-3d, Cu-3d, and S/Se-p orbitals near the Fermi level.
AI.conclusion: Mn substitution is an effective strategy for tuning the electronic properties of Cu2NiXY4 kesterites. The ability to control bandgap through doping makes these materials promising for optoelectronic devices requiring adjustable bandgaps.

title: Fractional Chern insulators in alternating twisted multilayer MoTe$_{2}$
summary: We study strongly correlated many-body states in alternating twisted trilayer and tetralayer MoTe$_{2}$. By sliding the top layer with respect to others and applying a perpendicular electric field, a variety of band structures can be realized. In many cases, the topmost hole band has unity Chern number and its quantum geometric properties can be tuned to some extent.
