Exact diagonalizations suggest that fractional Chern insulators are stabilized in certain parameter regimes but not in some regimes even when the band is topological. This contrast is attributed primarily to different quantum geometries as quantified by the trace condition. Our results demonstrate that sliding can serve as a useful knob for probing many-body states in moiré systems.

title: Temperature-driven sodium-ion dynamical-to-static crossover in the zig-zag ordered phase of Na$_{0.5}$CoO$_2$
comment: 17 pages, 12 figures, to appear in Physical Review B
summary: We employ polarization-resolved Raman spectroscopy combined with first-principles calculations to study the sodium-ion lattice dynamics in a sodium zig-zag ordered cobaltate compound Na$_{0.5}$CoO$_2$. We detect two sodium phonon modes for the first time, and their mode frequencies are consistent with first-principles phonon calculations based on an orthorhombic unit cell. We find that they appear below around $T^*\sim300\pm50$K with large linewidth broadening, much lower than the sodium zig-zag ordering temperature $T_\text{S}\sim460$K, and then narrow at lower temperatures. We interpret the sodium-phonon anomalies occurring at $T^*$ as a dynamical-to-static crossover involving mainly the motion of sodium ions. Our results suggest that the gradual freezing of the sodium ions and the well-defined static sodium-zigzag order below $T^*$ set the stage for the emergent electronic and magnetic orders in the CoO$_2$ layer of Na$_{0.5}$CoO$_2$.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates sodium-ion lattice dynamics in Na₀.₅CoO₂ using polarization-resolved Raman spectroscopy and first-principles calculations, discovering two sodium phonon modes that appear below ~300K with large linewidth broadening.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand the sodium-ion dynamics and their role in the emergent electronic and magnetic orders in Na₀.₅CoO₂, particularly the relationship between sodium zig-zag ordering and electronic properties.
AI.method: Polarization-resolved Raman spectroscopy combined with first-principles phonon calculations based on an orthorhombic unit cell.
AI.result: Two sodium phonon modes were detected for the first time, with mode frequencies consistent with first-principles calculations. These modes appear below T* ~ 300±50K with large linewidth broadening (lower than the sodium zig-zag ordering temperature T_S ~ 460K) and narrow at lower temperatures.
AI.conclusion: The sodium-phonon anomalies at T* represent a dynamical-to-static crossover involving sodium ion motion. The gradual freezing of sodium ions and the static sodium-zigzag order below T* set the stage for emergent electronic and magnetic orders in the CoO₂ layer.

title: Angular momentum splitter effect of $d$-wave axial phonons in orbital altermagnets
summary: We theoretically demonstrate that axial phonons, lattice vibration quanta carrying finite angular momentum, can host a $d$-wave angular momentum texture in orbital altermagnets in the absence of spin-orbit coupling. We consider a minimal electronic tight-binding model with $d$-wave loop-current order that breaks time-reversal symmetry. Within the Born-Oppenheimer approximation, we incorporate electron-phonon coupling via the molecular Berry curvature and show that the underlying $d$-wave orbital magnetic moment texture of the electronic state is transferred to the phonons without requiring the relativistic spin-orbit coupling. Our results expand the range of platforms available for engineering axial phonons and point to functionality unique to $d$-wave textures, including angular-momentum Seebeck and splitter effects, corresponding to longitudinal and transverse angular-momentum currents driven by a temperature gradient.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that axial phonons with d-wave angular momentum texture can emerge in orbital altermagnets without spin-orbit coupling, through electron-phonon coupling via molecular Berry curvature.
AI.motivation: To expand the range of platforms for engineering axial phonons and explore functionality unique to d-wave textures beyond relativistic effects.
AI.method: Minimal electronic tight-binding model with d-wave loop-current order; Born-Oppenheimer approximation; electron-phonon coupling via molecular Berry curvature.
AI.result: The d-wave orbital magnetic moment texture of the electronic state transfers to phonons without requiring spin-orbit coupling.
AI.conclusion: Provides new platforms for axial phonon engineering and predicts unique functionalities including angular-momentum Seebeck and splitter effects driven by temperature gradients.

title: Electron Beam Radiolysis-Assisted Growth of Rutile TiO2 Thin Films
summary: A new approach for growing crystalline thin films is developed that takes advantage of electron beam radiolysis being a constructive force to rearrange atoms into a crystalline structure. It is demonstrated that by irradiating the surface of a TiO2 film by an electron beam supplied by a reflection high energy electron diffraction (RHEED) gun inside the MBE chamber during growth, a crystalline film can be grown at much lower substrate temperatures, where deposited films typically appear amorphous. Here, rutile TiO2 films were grown using hybrid molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) allowing atomic level control of growth as well as an observation of radiolysis-driven crystallization. Analysis was carried out using a combination of SEM and atomic-resolution STEM imaging. It is also shown that by tuning the temperature of the substrate and the dose of the electron beam, the degree of crystallinity of the film can be controlled.

AI.tldr: A new electron beam radiolysis-driven crystallization method enables growth of crystalline TiO2 thin films at lower substrate temperatures using hybrid MBE, with crystallinity controllable via electron dose and temperature tuning.
AI.motivation: Traditional thin film growth requires high substrate temperatures to achieve crystalline structures; at lower temperatures, deposited films typically remain amorphous. This study seeks to overcome this limitation by using electron beam radiolysis as a constructive force to drive crystallization.
AI.method: Rutile TiO2 films were grown using hybrid molecular beam epitaxy (MBE). During growth, the film surface was irradiated using an electron beam from a reflection high energy electron diffraction (RHEED) gun. The substrate temperature and electron beam dose were varied to control the crystallization process. Film analysis was performed using SEM and atomic-resolution STEM imaging.
AI.result: Crystalline TiO2 films were successfully grown at significantly lower substrate temperatures than normally required. The degree of crystallinity could be controlled by tuning both the substrate temperature and the electron beam dose, demonstrating the ability to precisely control film properties during growth.
AI.conclusion: Electron beam radiolysis can serve as an effective constructive force for thin film crystallization, enabling low-temperature growth of crystalline materials. This approach provides a new degree of control over thin film crystallinity through independent tuning of substrate temperature and electron beam parameters.

title: Lecture Notes: The two-dimensional electron Wigner crystal -- What's old and what's new?
comment: 27 pages, 12 figures, ~12 jokes. Thanks to Sandeep Joy for proofreading
summary: These are lecture notes created for a short lecture series at the 2026 CTEQ Summer School at Penn State. They are written in a conversational and informal style. The goal of these notes is to introduce and review a smattering of old and new ideas about the Wigner crystal (the solid phase of the two-dimensional electron system) in the context of recent experiments. Particular emphasis is given to the semiclassical description of the Wigner crystal, its quantum melting transition, its spin order, and the ways in which the Wigner crystal can be modified by Berry curvature.

AI.tldr: Lecture notes reviewing old and new ideas about Wigner crystals (2D electron solid) in context of recent experiments, covering semiclassical description, quantum melting transition, spin order, and Berry curvature effects.
AI.motivation: Introduce and review Wigner crystal physics for students at CTEQ Summer School, bridging fundamental concepts with recent experimental advances.
AI.method: Pedagogical review using conversational/informal style; covering semiclassical approaches, quantum phase transitions, ordered phases, and topological effects (Berry curvature).
AI.result: Comprehensive educational material on Wigner crystal physics including semiclassical description, quantum melting, spin ordering, and modified phases via Berry curvature.
AI.conclusion: Provides accessible introduction to Wigner crystal physics combining historical perspective with modern experimental context, suitable for graduate students and beginners in the field.

title: Ultrafast altermagnetophononics
summary: Altermagnets feature symmetry-dictated nontrivial spin splitting in electronic structure promising for next-generation spintronics, yet their ultrafast dynamical manipulation remains largely unexplored. Here, we establish altermagnetophononics as an efficient route for magnetic control via selective symmetry breaking induced by coherent phonons. Using the prototypical altermagnet $α$-MnTe as an example, we demonstrate that the targeted excitation of B$_{1g}$ mode selectively lifts the symmetry constraints protecting altermagnetism (AM), driving ultrafast transition into a transient compensated ferrimagnetic (cFiM) phase characterized by a global spin splitting without net magnetization. Further, we show that the proposed mechanism is broadly applicable by demonstrating a multi-mode symmetry breaking pathway, and by realizing a ferrimagnetic order with reversible magnetic moment in metallic CrSb. These findings elucidate the potential to obtain desirable nonequilibrium properties in altermagnets via coherent phononic control over their spin splittings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "altermagnetophononics" as a method to control altermagnetic materials using coherent phonons. By exciting specific phonon modes in α-MnTe, researchers selectively break symmetry and induce a transient compensated ferrimagnetic (cFiM) phase. The approach is demonstrated to be broadly applicable in metallic CrSb, showing reversible magnetic moment control.
AI.motivation: Altermagnets have symmetry-dictated nontrivial spin splitting promising for spintronics, but their ultrafast dynamical manipulation remains unexplored. This work aims to develop an efficient route for magnetic control via selective symmetry breaking induced by coherent phonons.
AI.method: Using α-MnTe as the prototypical altermagnet, the researchers selectively excited the B₁g phonon mode to lift symmetry constraints protecting altermagnetism (AM). They also demonstrated multi-mode symmetry breaking pathways and tested the approach in metallic CrSb.
AI.result: Targeted B₁g mode excitation drives an ultrafast transition into a transient compensated ferrimagnetic (cFiM) phase characterized by global spin splitting without net magnetization. The mechanism proved broadly applicable, achieving ferrimagnetic order with reversible magnetic moment in CrSb.
AI.conclusion: Coherent phononic control over spin splittings provides a powerful pathway to obtain desirable nonequilibrium properties in altermagnets, establishing altermagnetophononics as an efficient route for magnetic manipulation in next-generation spintronics.

title: $\texttt{iNORG}$: An open-source quantum impurity solver package based on the natural orbitals renormalization group
summary: In the context of dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) calculations for strongly correlated electron systems, quantum impurity solvers play a central computational role in treating correlated lattice models and realistic materials. Consequently, developing efficient and robust quantum impurity solvers remains a key challenge. In this paper, we present an open-source quantum impurity solver package based on the natural orbitals renormalization group (NORG) method, dubbed $\texttt{iNORG}$. This software delivers high accuracy with reduced computational cost by optimizing the bath representation using natural orbitals and incorporating advanced features such as efficient Hilbert space selection and efficient algorithms for computing Green's functions. We first introduce the basic principle of the NORG method and then discuss the implementation details. The software framework, major features, and installation procedure for $\texttt{iNORG}$ are explained as well. Finally, several simple examples are presented to demonstrate the usage of $\texttt{iNORG}$.

AI.tldr: iNORG is an open-source quantum impurity solver package based on the natural orbitals renormalization group (NORG) method for DMFT calculations. It achieves high accuracy with reduced computational cost through optimized bath representation using natural orbitals, efficient Hilbert space selection, and efficient algorithms for computing Green's functions.
AI.motivation: Developing efficient and robust quantum impurity solvers is a key challenge in DMFT calculations for strongly correlated electron systems, as they play a central computational role in treating correlated lattice models and realistic materials.
AI.method: The NORG method optimizes bath representation using natural orbitals. The software incorporates efficient Hilbert space selection and efficient algorithms for computing Green's functions.
AI.result: The software delivers high accuracy with reduced computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Several simple examples are presented to demonstrate the usage of iNORG.

title: Revealing the MoS2 Growth Mechanism in Chemical Vapor Deposition: Real-Time Imaging and Statistical Analysis
summary: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) is a promising method for scalable synthesis of two-dimensional transitional metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) such as MoS2, but challenges in reproducibility and controllability persist due to an incomplete understanding of their dynamic growth mechanisms. While in-situ characterization methods could provide valuable insights, it remains challenging to track a large ensemble of crystals to enable quantitative, statistical analysis. Here, we address this gap by developing and applying a semi-automated image processing pipeline to analyze in-situ optical microscopy footage of MoS2 growth. This framework enables the high-throughput reconstruction of complete growth trajectories for over 400 individual crystals from a single experiment. Our statistical analysis demonstrates that MoS2 crystallization is governed by an edge-attachment-limited mechanism rather than by precursor diffusion. Furthermore, MoS2 crystals exhibit non-competitive growth, indicating that precursor supply does not limit the growth of neighboring flakes until physical impingement occurs. These findings provide direct, quantitative evidence that advances the fundamental understanding of TMD growth, establishing a powerful methodology for rational optimization of the CVD growth of two-dimensional materials.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a semi-automated image processing pipeline to analyze in-situ optical microscopy footage of MoS2 growth during chemical vapor deposition (CVD), enabling high-throughput reconstruction of growth trajectories for over 400 individual crystals to determine the growth mechanism.
AI.motivation: CVD is a promising method for scalable synthesis of 2D TMDs like MoS2, but challenges in reproducibility and controllability persist due to incomplete understanding of dynamic growth mechanisms. Existing in-situ characterization methods struggle to track large crystal ensembles for quantitative, statistical analysis.
AI.method: The researchers developed and applied a semi-automated image processing pipeline to analyze in-situ optical microscopy footage of MoS2 growth. This enabled high-throughput reconstruction of complete growth trajectories for over 400 individual crystals from a single experiment.
AI.result: Statistical analysis demonstrated that MoS2 crystallization is governed by an edge-attachment-limited mechanism rather than by precursor diffusion. Additionally, MoS2 crystals exhibit non-competitive growth, indicating that precursor supply does not limit the growth of neighboring flakes until physical impingement occurs.
AI.conclusion: These findings provide direct, quantitative evidence that advances the fundamental understanding of TMD growth, establishing a powerful methodology for rational optimization of CVD growth of 2D materials.

title: Thermodynamic and electrical transport properties of the half-Heusler plumbide TbAuPb
comment: 9 Pages, 6 figures, 2 Pages supplementary information, Published in Physical Review Materials
summary: Structural, thermodynamic and electrical transport properties of TbAuPb were investigated on single crystals. The compound was found to crystallize with the cubic MgAgAs-type structure characteristic of half-Heusler materials. It orders antiferromagnetically at TN = 5 K and undergoes a transition into a different antiferromagnetic phase emerging in high magnetic fields. Electrical transport in TbAuPb exhibits a multiband character, with a predominance of hole-like carriers. Angular magnetoresistance evolves systematically with applied magnetic field and changes its symmetry near the spin-reorientation transition, highlighting strong coupling between the charge transport and the magnetic order. The results of first-principles calculations indicate that TbAuPb is a band inverted semimetal in the non-magnetic state, which becomes topologically trivial in the field-induced ferromagnetic state.

AI.tldr: TbAuPb is a cubic half-Heusler compound (MgAgAs-type) exhibiting antiferromagnetic ordering at 5 K, with a field-induced transition to a different antiferromagnetic phase. It shows multiband electrical transport with hole-like dominance and strong coupling between magnetoresistance and magnetic order. First-principles calculations reveal it as a band-inverted semimetal in the non-magnetic state that becomes topologically trivial under field-induced ferromagnetism.
AI.motivation: The study aims to investigate the structural, thermodynamic, and electrical transport properties of TbAuPb as a potential topological material, exploring the interplay between magnetic order and electronic band topology in half-Heusler compounds.
AI.method: Single crystal growth, combined with electrical transport measurements (temperature/field-dependent resistivity, angular magnetoresistance), thermodynamic measurements, and first-principles electronic structure calculations.
AI.result: TbAuPb crystallizes in the cubic MgAgAs-type structure. It exhibits antiferromagnetic ordering at TN = 5 K and undergoes a spin-reorientation transition in high magnetic fields. Electrical transport shows multiband behavior with hole-like carriers. Angular magnetoresistance evolves with field and changes symmetry at the magnetic transition, indicating strong coupling between charge transport and magnetic order. Calculations confirm a band-inverted semimetal topology in the non-magnetic state.
AI.conclusion: TbAuPb represents a candidate topological material where magnetic order can tune the band topology, transitioning from a band-inverted semimetal to a topologically trivial state under field-induced ferromagnetism. The strong magnetoresponse and coupling between transport and magnetic order make it interesting for further investigation of topological magnetism in half-Heusler systems.

title: Chemical short-range order controls deformation pathways in a complex concentrated alloy
comment: 25 pages, 7 figures, 8 supplementary information figures, 42 references
summary: Chemical short-range order (CSRO) is an intrinsic feature of complex concentrated alloys (CCAs), yet its influence on deformation mechanisms is controversial because of the inconclusive state of concurrent CSRO quantification during deformation. Here, we provide experimental evidence that CSRO acts as an intrinsic thermodynamic state variable governing stacking-fault energetics and deformation pathways in a Co30Cr40Ni30 alloy. By comparing quenched (CSRO-lean) and aged (CSRO-enriched) conditions with equivalent grain structure and phase constitution, we isolate the influence of atomic-scale chemical ordering on mechanical behavior. Calorimetry confirms reversible CSRO formation, while synchrotron X-ray diffraction and electron microscopy reveal that CSRO suppresses deformation-induced fcc-hcp martensitic transformation at both room and cryogenic temperatures. Despite differences in transformation dynamics, the macroscopic tensile response is still broadly similar. Atomistic simulations show that CSRO increases both stable and unstable stacking-fault energies, raising the energetic barrier for partial-dislocation activity and stabilizing the fcc lattice against transformation. Together, the experimental and computational results establish CSRO as an added degree of freedom for tuning stacking-fault energetics and controlling deformation pathways in complex concentrated alloys.

AI.tldr: Chemical short‑range order (CSRO) acts as a thermodynamic variable that raises stacking‑fault energies and suppresses the fcc‑hcp martensitic transformation in a Co30Cr40Ni30 alloy, providing a new lever for tuning deformation pathways.
AI.motivation: The influence of CSRO on deformation mechanisms of complex concentrated alloys is debated; this work seeks to resolve the controversy by isolating the effect of atomic‑scale chemical ordering on stacking‑fault energetics and transformation behavior.
AI.method: Quenched (CSRO‑lean) and aged (CSRO‑enriched) Co30Cr40Ni30 samples with identical grain structure and phase composition were compared using calorimetry, synchrotron X‑ray diffraction, electron microscopy, and atomistic simulations.
AI.result: CSRO formation is reversible; it increases both stable and unstable stacking‑fault energies, raises the barrier for partial‑dislocation activity, and suppresses the deformation‑induced fcc‑hcp martensitic transformation at room and cryogenic temperatures, while the macroscopic tensile response remains similar.
AI.conclusion: CSRO can be treated as an additional degree of freedom for tailoring stacking‑fault energetics and controlling deformation pathways in complex concentrated alloys.

title: Unified Terahertz Framework for Magnetic and Lattice Responses Reveals an Elusive Ordering Transition in Gd$_2$Ru$_2$O$_7$
summary: Magnetic order in materials combining localized rare-earth moments with itinerant transition-metal sublattices generates internal fields whose lattice imprint is rarely accessed directly. In Gd pyrochlore ruthenate, we find that a single terahertz spectrum resolves an exchange-split Gd$^{3+}$ mode and an optical phonon. Their coupled evolution quantifies the internal field, oriented as predicted for cluster-multipolar order, and reveals a Gd-ordering transition elusive to bulk thermodynamic probes, establishing a unified framework for accessing magnetic and lattice responses in correlated quantum materials.

AI.tldr: This study uses terahertz spectroscopy to investigate magnetic order in Gd pyrochlore ruthenate, resolving an exchange-split Gd3+ mode and optical phonon that reveal internal fields and a previously undetected Gd-ordering transition.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to directly access internal fields generated by magnetic order in materials combining localized rare-earth moments with itinerant transition-metal sublattices, which are rarely accessible through conventional bulk thermodynamic probes.
AI.method: The researchers used terahertz spectroscopy to study Gd pyrochlore ruthenate, examining the coupled evolution of an exchange-split Gd3+ mode and an optical phonon.
AI.result: The study successfully quantified the internal field orientation, which matches predictions for cluster-multipolar order, and revealed a Gd-ordering transition that was previously elusive to bulk thermodynamic probes.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a unified framework for accessing both magnetic and lattice responses in correlated quantum materials through terahertz spectroscopy.

title: Dolomite Mineral-Inspired Equilateral Triangular-Lattice Magnets for Quantum Magnetism
summary: Equilateral triangular lattice magnets provide a versatile materials platform for exploring exotic quantum spin phenomena, while their field-tunable magnetic entropy offers opportunities for low-temperature adiabatic demagnetization refrigeration. Inspired by the natural mineral, we proposed a chemical strategy to achieve equilateral TL magnets, leveraging the high crystal symmetry of a large family of dolomite-type materials. As typical examples, the dolomite-type materials SnM(BO3)2 (M = Co, Mn) were synthesized, and structural analysis reveals that Co2+ and Mn2+ ions form equilateral triangular lattices with an A-B-C stacking fashion. The magnetic susceptibilities and specific heat measurements reveal dominant antiferromagnetic interactions, with Neel temperatures of 0.49K for SnCo(BO3)2 and 0.96K for SnMn(BO3)2, respectively. Our results establish the dolomite-type M'M(X)2 (M'and M sites allow various valence states, e.g., +4/+2 or +3/+3; X = CO32- or BO33-) system as a chemically flexible and structurally perfect material platform for exploring frustrated magnetism and low-temperature magnetocaloric applications.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the synthesis and characterization of equilateral triangular lattice magnets (SnM(BO3)2, M=Co, Mn) using a dolomite-type materials platform. These materials show antiferromagnetic ordering at low temperatures (0.49K for Co, 0.96K for Mn) and offer potential for exploring frustrated magnetism and magnetocaloric applications.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a versatile materials platform for exploring exotic quantum spin phenomena and field-tunable magnetic entropy for low-temperature adiabatic demagnetization refrigeration, inspired by natural minerals.
AI.method: The researchers proposed a chemical strategy leveraging the high crystal symmetry of dolomite-type materials to achieve equilateral triangular lattice magnets. They synthesized dolomite-type materials SnM(BO3)2 (M = Co, Mn) and performed structural analysis, magnetic susceptibility, and specific heat measurements.
AI.result: The structural analysis revealed that Co2+ and Mn2+ ions form equilateral triangular lattices with an A-B-C stacking fashion. Magnetic measurements showed dominant antiferromagnetic interactions with Neel temperatures of 0.49K for SnCo(BO3)2 and 0.96K for SnMn(BO3)2.
AI.conclusion: The dolomite-type M'M(X)2 system is established as a chemically flexible and structurally perfect material platform for exploring frustrated magnetism and low-temperature magnetocaloric applications, with various valence states possible at M' and M sites.

title: Using superpixels for interpretable feature reduction in large 2D diffraction datasets
summary: Large 2D diffraction datasets, consisting of hundreds or thousands of measurements, are commonly acquired with 4D-STEM electron diffraction or at synchrotron X-ray beamlines. Machine learning and artificial intelligence offer great promise for analyzing these datasets. However, the sheer volume of data presents a significant data processing bottleneck. Cropping the detector and pixel binning are standard ways to reduce data size. Here we propose grouping and averaging pixels into superpixels of variable area. High-information regions are sampled densely, while low-information areas are collected into larger superpixels. In the process, symmetries in the data are captured and exploited, making this approach suitable for preprocessing 2D diffraction data. We compare two variance-minimizing methods: K-means clustering (top-down) and agglomerative clustering (bottom-up) and demonstrate superior scaling and interpretability for the bottom-up method. As these methods are distance-based, we demonstrate that the construction of superpixels can be accelerated using Gaussian random projection. Finally we show over 100-fold acceleration for phase mapping with Non-negative matrix factorization on a 4D-STEM dataset when superpixels are used as a preprocessing step.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a superpixel-based preprocessing method for large 2D diffraction datasets from 4D-STEM and synchrotron X-ray beamlines. By grouping pixels into variable-area superpixels based on information density, data size is reduced while preserving key structural information. The bottom-up agglomerative clustering method outperforms top-down K-means in scalability and interpretability. Using Gaussian random projection accelerates the process. Results show over 100-fold speedup for Non-negative matrix factorization phase mapping.
AI.motivation: The massive volume of data from modern 4D-STEM and synchrotron X-ray diffraction creates a significant processing bottleneck. Standard approaches like cropping and pixel binning are crude and may lose important information. The motivation is to develop a smarter downsampling approach that preserves high-information regions while compressing low-information areas.
AI.method: The method involves creating superpixels by grouping adjacent pixels based on similarity. Two variance-minimizing clustering approaches are compared: (1) K-means clustering (top-down approach) and (2) agglomerative clustering (bottom-up approach). Gaussian random projection is used to accelerate distance calculations. The superpixel output serves as preprocessing for Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) phase mapping.
AI.result: Agglomerative (bottom-up) clustering shows superior scaling and interpretability compared to K-means. Gaussian random projection accelerates superpixel construction. When applied as preprocessing for NMF phase mapping on a 4D-STEM dataset, the method achieves over 100-fold acceleration.
AI.conclusion: Superpixel-based preprocessing is an effective strategy for handling large 2D diffraction datasets. The bottom-up agglomerative clustering method is recommended over K-means. This approach enables practical analysis of massive diffraction datasets while preserving critical structural information, making it highly valuable for high-throughput materials characterization.

title: The WEST code for large-scale excited-state materials simulations
summary: We present WEST, an open-source plane-wave pseudopotential code for large-scale excited-state materials simulations, and describe its theoretical foundations, software architecture, and capabilities. WEST implements full-frequency GW, quantum defect embedding theory, the Bethe-Salpeter equation, and time-dependent density functional theory within a common algorithmic framework that avoids the explicit computation of virtual electronic states. By combining density functional and density matrix perturbation theory, low-rank representations of the dielectric screening and exact exchange, and localization techniques, WEST achieves favorable computational scaling with system size. The code supports the calculation of quasi-particle and neutral excitation energies, optical and photoluminescence spectra, excited-state forces, and non-adiabatic couplings, with interoperable workflows connecting to quantum chemistry, vibronic coupling, and quantum computing packages. A hierarchical parallelization strategy and GPU acceleration deliver near-ideal strong scaling to thousands of GPUs, enabling accurate excited-state simulations of systems with more than a thousand atoms. Representative applications, spanning the full optical cycle of solid-state spin defects, self-trapped excitons in metal-halide perovskites, and the optical response of liquid water and ice, demonstrate the accuracy and versatility of the code across diverse material classes. The capabilities implemented in WEST establish the code as a scalable platform for predictive excited-state simulations, high-throughput materials discovery, and the generation of high-fidelity datasets for machine learning in computational materials science.

AI.tldr: WEST is an open-source plane-wave pseudopotential code for large-scale excited-state materials simulations, implementing GW, quantum defect embedding, BSE, and TDDFT with favorable scaling to thousands of atoms and GPUs.
AI.motivation: The need for scalable, accurate excited-state simulations for predictive materials discovery, high-throughput screening, and machine learning dataset generation across diverse material classes.
AI.method: Common algorithmic framework avoiding explicit virtual states; density functional and density matrix perturbation theory; low-rank representations of dielectric screening and exact exchange; localization techniques; hierarchical parallelization and GPU acceleration.
AI.result: Near-ideal strong scaling to thousands of GPUs; enables accurate simulations of systems with >1000 atoms; demonstrated applications include solid-state spin defects, self-trapped excitons in perovskites, and optical response of water/ice.
AI.conclusion: WEST establishes itself as a scalable platform for predictive excited-state simulations, high-throughput materials discovery, and generation of high-fidelity ML datasets in computational materials science.

title: Orbital Hybridization Induces Giant Cubic Rashba Effect at Cu/WO$_{3}$ Interface
summary: Harnessing Rashba spin-orbit interaction and related spintronic functionalities has traditionally relied on metallic surfaces or interfaces containing elemental heavy metals. Here, using first-principles calculations and Cu(001)/WO$_3$(001) as a model heterostructure, we show that interfacing a light metal, Cu, with a band insulator, WO$_3$, yields an interface state that exhibits a robust Rashba spin splitting arising from the interplay between linear and cubic Rashba effects. The spin splitting is driven by the strong spin-orbit coupling of W atoms and enabled by W-Cu orbital hybridization at the interface. The cubic Rashba contribution asymptotically grows with Cu thickness and can be explained in terms of cross-coupling between the vacuum/Cu and Cu/WO$_3$ interfaces. This interfacial cross-coupling, however, diminishes at larger Cu thicknesses, allowing us to extract the intrinsic cubic Rashba parameter, which has a giant value of approximately -1.93 eV $Å^3$. In contrast, the linear Rashba parameter is only weakly affected by this cross-coupling and varies from approximately 0.30 to 0.49 eV Å. We further show that sizable linear and cubic Rashba effects persist across several interface geometries and Cu surface orientations, including (110) and (111). Our work identifies the Cu/WO$_3$ interface as a novel light-metal/heavy-element-based oxide platform for exploring the rich spectrum of Rashba physics, including linear and nonlinear spin-orbit phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that interfacing a light metal (Cu) with a band insulator (WO3) creates a robust Rashba spin splitting at the interface, driven by W atoms' strong spin-orbit coupling and W-Cu orbital hybridization. The system exhibits both linear and cubic Rashba effects, with the cubic contribution growing with Cu thickness due to cross-coupling between interfaces.
AI.motivation: Traditionally, Rashba spin-orbit interaction has relied on heavy metals. This work aims to explore a novel platform using light metals combined with heavy-element oxides to harness Rashba physics without traditional heavy metal layers.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were performed on Cu(001)/WO3(001) heterostructures to investigate the electronic structure and spin-orbit effects at the interface.
AI.result: The study found a giant intrinsic cubic Rashba parameter of approximately -1.93 eV Å³ and a linear Rashba parameter ranging from 0.30 to 0.49 eV Å. These effects persist across multiple interface geometries and Cu surface orientations (110 and 111).
AI.conclusion: The Cu/WO3 interface represents a novel light-metal/heavy-element-based oxide platform for exploring Rashba physics and nonlinear spin-orbit phenomena, offering new avenues for spintronic applications.
